title: Suppression of Quasiparticle Poisoning to $10^{-11}$ Levels in Superconducting Qubits via Infrared Shielding
authors: Wei-En Lin, Chen-Hsun Ma, Erh-Hsiang Yeh, Wei-Lun Peng, Yu-Sen Wei, Hsi-Sheng Goan, Cen-Shawn Wu, Chung-Ting Ke, Yung-Fu Chen, Chii-Dong Chen
abstract: Quasiparticle poisoning bottlenecks superconducting qubits, limiting coherence and the scalability of quantum processors. In this work, we systematically investigate quasiparticle poisoning in superconducting qubits under three infrared (IR) shielding configurations, ranging from a dedicated multi-layer design to a simplified implementation. By measuring quasiparticle-induced parity switching, we demonstrate a suppression of the switching rate by over four orders of magnitude via the implementation of improved shielding. In the best configuration, the rate decreases over time following cooldown and reaches 0.069$\,$Hz on day 34, corresponding to an anticipated quasiparticle density per Cooper pair of $1.88\times10^{-11}$. To our knowledge, this represents the lowest quasiparticle density reported in the literature to date. The remaining quasiparticle population is likely dominated by sporadic phonon bursts stemming from mechanical stress release in the on-chip films, as well as from the surrounding environment. The effective qubit temperature follows the phonon bath down to 17$\,$mK, enabling initialization errors of $\sim 0.01\%$ for 3$\,$GHz qubits. These results demonstrate that proper IR shielding and thermalization are essential for suppressing quasiparticle poisoning and enabling high-coherence, scalable superconducting qubit systems.
summary: The authors investigated quasiparticle poisoning in superconducting qubits using various infrared (IR) shielding methods. By measuring parity switching rates over extended periods, they demonstrated that improved shielding can suppress decoherence sources by many orders of magnitude. This work establishes a new low benchmark for quasiparticle density, proving the necessity of meticulous environmental control for scalable quantum computing.
main_problem: Quasiparticle poisoning severely limits coherence times and scalability in superconducting qubits.
main_result: Implementing advanced infrared (IR) shielding techniques suppressed the quasiparticle switching rate by over four orders of magnitude, achieving a record low density estimate of $1.88 	imes 10^{-11}$ per Cooper pair.
method: The study measured quasiparticle-induced parity switching rates ($\Gamma_p$) over long cooldown periods and characterized qubit temperature ($T_q$).
model_or_system: Floating transmon superconducting qubits are used, which are susceptible to decoherence from environmental IR radiation and stray quasiparticles.
key_observables: Parity-switching rate ($\Gamma_p$), effective qubit temperature ($T_q$), quasiparticle density per Cooper pair ($x_{qp}$), and energy relaxation time ($T_1$).
important_parameters: Qubit frequency $\approx 3$ GHz; achieved $x_{qp} \approx 1.88 	imes 10^{-11}$; effective temperature down to $17	ext{ mK}$.
main_assumptions: The remaining quasiparticle population is likely dominated by sporadic phonon bursts from mechanical stress or the environment.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, time evolution of $\Gamma_p$ showing decay over days, and plots comparing $T_q$ to the phonon bath temperature ($T_{MX}$).
paper_structure: The work systematically compares three IR shielding configurations (A, B, C) by measuring parity switching rates ($\Gamma_p$) and characterizing thermalization effects ($T_q$) over time to determine the optimal environment for high-coherence operation.
why_interesting: This research provides crucial experimental benchmarks on environmental noise mitigation in superconducting qubits, directly impacting the feasibility of scaling quantum processors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup of Configuration A. (a) Qubit measurement wiring. The device image shows a floating trans- mon qubit coupled to a readout resonator, which is in turn coupled to an open transmission line. The chip is enclosed within a dedicated multilayer shielding assembly (yellow box) and the mixing-plate radiation shield provided by Bluefors (gray box). Two IR filters are implemented on both the input and output sides of the open transmission line. (b) Detailed schematic of shielding Configuration A. The shielding struc- ture is concentrically arranged from inside to outside as fol- lows: a copper enclosure, an aluminum enclosure, and a mu- metal shield. The copper enclosure is...

FIG. 2. Parity-switching measurements. (a) PSD of the parity-switching signal for Q2 measured on Days 1 and 33. The spectra are fitted with a Lorentzian to extract Γp = 2.09 Hz (Day 1) and 0.080 Hz (Day 33). (b) Extracted Γp vs. time for all three qubits over 36 days, fitted to a power law with exponents −1.04, −0.89, and −1.28 for Q1–Q3, re- spectively. (c) Γp vs. P1 for Q2 at several gate charges ng (Day 15), from which Γ1 p is extrapolated. (d) Corresponding Γ1 p as a function of ng.

FIG. 3. Effective qubit temperature. (a) Qubit state readout for Q2 on Day 20, yielding P1 = 0.0218% and a corresponding Tq = 16.8 mK. (b) Tq vs. time for all three qubits, with TMX shown for comparison. (c) Tq as a function of TMX for all three qubits. The legend in (c) applies to both (b) and (c). The dashed line indicates Tq = TMX.
FIG. 4. Histogram of T1 for Q2 on Day 24. The distribution has a mean of 402 µs and a standard deviation of 60.7 µs. The inset shows the longest measured T1, reaching 600 µs.
